transcript.text: I've got friends that live in Oakland.
Oh, do you?
Mm It's honestly so nice.
I'm Canadian originally, and I moved here like seven years ago, and I would almost never come to Oakland.
then when we're looking for kind of a longer place, you know, a longer-term place to stay, we came here and were like, holy crap.
It's a cool spot.
I like how close it is to SF, but it's not in SF, and you're not dealing with SF things.
Yeah, exactly.
It's a nice blend between here and LA in my head.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, it's really nice to meet you.
You know, Bridget shared some context.
Maybe I could just give a quick intro, but we'd love to hear more about you.
Yeah.
And go from there.
That's cool.
Sounds good.
Cool.
So I lead GoToMarket at GrowthX, which is essentially two parts.
One part, I have kind of a book of clients, some of the bigger ones.
You might have heard of Webflow and Abnormal Security.
And we have...
I have a few smaller startups.
So that's one half of my role here.
And then the other half is to basically grow growthx, both from top of funnel, which is more on the marketing and sales side.
And also, I mean, the main kind of reason for why I have some clients is to figure out how to make that more efficient.
And a big part of that is like, you know, at growthx, we're really trying to build like almost a kind of agency for the age of AI.
Yeah.
And all these ways, you know, agencies are run today.
It doesn't really make sense now with AI.

And part of, you know, why we wanted to talk to you and part of where my head's at usually is just like, okay, what's exactly breaking when you have three people handle 10 clients instead of, you know, five?
And how do you kind of make that scalable and continue to scale that with just like automation?
Sometimes it's not even AI, you know?
Yeah.
Yeah.
So that's what I do here in the, my background is basically.
That, exactly that, go-to-market growth, leading that for tech companies.
I've been at a few startups like Kite and Bardeen, if you've heard of them, and also led growth at the firm.
I don't know if you've tried to buy a Peloton, but you'll see a firm at checkout.
And then I also had my own startup as a founder.
Back by YC, it was like a dev tool startup that ultimately ended up folding.
Yeah, that's about me.
And then how I joined these was, I don't know if you've used any of these AI tools, but there was a big one called Aerofs.
And I met Marcel at an event where, I mean, basically they invited me because I was like their, you know, number one power user, essentially.
Yeah.
Leading growth at my last company, and it seemed like a no-brainer to work with them.
Cool.
Thanks for that background.
Cool, no problem.
Sounds like you've got a lot of different flavors throughout your career.
yeah.
I think that's why, you know, it helps to be here.
There's a lot of just, like, figuring things out from first principles.
Yeah, that's part of what, like, it really excites me about this role.
Because I've kind of worked with the big corpos for the past couple of years, past five years or so.
I moved up here to Washington for my master's in entrepreneurship.
So I studied how to start companies, how to build from the ground up in my master's degree, kind of started my own little ventures while I was in that.
I ran, like, a dog services company out of my apartment to kind of help pay the bills and, you know, get my feet wet with official documentation and taxes and all that kind of stuff.
So it was a great experience.
And then kind of following the degree, the adjunct professor in the entrepreneurship program that I was a teaching assistant for joined a local company to start a new product line.
And he was like, hey, I'm working with you.
Come be my right hand man.
This is eight years ago now, I think.
Like, seven or eight years ago.
So CS was a very new concept at that point.
was very novice-level understanding within organizations of what goes into that.
So it was really cool to kind of dive into the deep end, build out a completely new product line.

This was a hardware and software mix, so I had to learn the networking and all the cabling and stuff for this hardware, but also support a SaaS backend and introduce a new SaaS backend to a team that had only worked with this on-prem.
solution before.
So it was really quite an extensive first role to dive into.
I kind of touched every angle of the business, working with marketing, product, engineering.
was F5?
No, this was Avanti Markets.
Oh, Avanti Markets.
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah, so that was my first role.
was kind of like everything under the sun that could go into customer operations.
And then, you know, after like three years there, they got acquired.
My boss was deemed redundant, so they let him go, and he was like the whole reason I was there.
So I was like, no better time to look for something new.
And I wanted to dive a little deeper into the weeds of the data, like what supports good customer operations in the first place.
Integrations, data pipelines, working with different warehouses, and dashboard builders like Tableau or native to CRM like Gainsight or Tatango or whatnot.
Right.
So it was a lot of exposure there to like the building blocks, the foundational tech stack kind of work.
And then all of that kind of culminated in my last role, the program manager role at F5.
I took that because I thought it was maybe going to be a little like more strategic level than it actually turned out to be.
There were a lot of leaders at the table.
I didn't really have a voice, and I didn't feel like I was really being utilized to my best value.
So hence me looking for that kind of role.
Now and taking a step away from F5, just a little too administrative for what I was looking for.
Got it.
And when you're at Ignite, like, how big was that company?
Ignite went from 800 when I started to 1000 while I was there.
So pretty good amount of growth.
And then Avanti when you're there, it looks like that one's a little bit smaller.
Oh, tiny.
Yeah, it was 15 people when I started and we had 35 when they got acquired and I left.
Got it.
Okay, and they got acquired.
Who did they get acquired by?
365 Retail Markets.
Interesting.
Yeah, they kind of, if you've ever seen like a self-service shop like at an airport or something with like a kiosk that you just self-check out, right?
That's the whole product that Avanti and 365 sold was unattended retail is how they called it.
Got it.
So was there, I mean, just like those two companies, like pretty opposite.
Like, oh, yeah.
How was your experience?
How
Did you have a preference?
Why?
I think for me, the preference comes in with the team size, the growth stage of the company, rather than the product that they're selling.
I'm really good with creative and critical thinking and collaborating in that sense.
So any place that I can sit at a table with other smart people and strategize and build good solutions from the ground up, that's where I'm going to thrive.
Coming into a space where all the tools are already decided and I'm picking up workflows and not really able to influence or alter them in any way, that's very much like what F5 had going on.
And I thought I was going to have a little more leverage to say, this is a way to do it, but it's maybe not the best way for us at this scale and this size to be doing it.
Yeah, I'm on the same page as you, for sure.
I was also part of a company that got acquired and just like...
Nope, this is not what I'm interested in.
Yeah, I want get back to my entrepreneurial roots from my master's degree.
For sure, growthx is that.
I know a lot of people are like, oh yeah, you got many startups within a startup or whatever.
I think that's the case for us, for sure.
We're spinning up new work streams that could be companies in themselves, especially because of how much revenue they have, they already are.
And we're doing that literally every week.
That's exciting.
So I guess, is there any customer ops type of project you've done recently, somewhat, that might look like something that we might want to do or relates to what we're struggling with at the moment?
Hard to tell immediately without being in the business and understanding the day-to-day.
But...

I would say at F5, one of my larger programs was building out the customer journey, basically from pre-sales through renewals and upsell and expansion, working within the contract cycle that F5 had, which was one year or three years, understanding what is the optimal amount of time for deployment and how do we find the right metrics for successful onboarding and stuff like that.
And F5's product, were you doing that for a particular product line?
Yeah, it was their SaaS product specifically.
was a new, they introduced their SaaS suite like three years ago.
And this year was like, this past year I was there was a huge growth year for the SaaS business.
They wanted to shift 80% of customers to the SaaS offering and start moving them off the on-prem solution that F5 started with.
And this is like everything?
I mean, I'm just on their website now.
I'm looking at solutions.
So they have like, you know, different products.
Yeah, they've taken most of their offerings.
them into a cloud solution, like a package deal, which is what was so compelling about it.
But it also introduced a lot of challenges to building out customer success metrics and measurements.
And you've got new telemetry, new data flows coming through, different integrations that need to work.

Well, I'm just looking at, okay, so like by solution area, like I don't know how they package their products, but like they have, I guess if they have a zero trust thing, they have a web app API production, I guess that could look like.
Like, I don't know, I guess we, I see Cloudflare's thing, you know, when I click a website too much or something.
Yeah.
It's a clear, secure multi-cloud networking.
So like the part, because I actually just want to learn like about that journey, like which, which one of these products did you kind of do that journey for?
Yeah, the SaaS product was predominantly DDoS protection.
Okay, very much like what Cloudflare does.
Yes, exactly what Cloudflare does.
Uh, and then you also got, um...
There was some like anonymizer that was involved in like the token chain of like validation.
So it was a little more technical than I could speak to.
But I think the DDoS and firewall was like the big thing.

And having like an online platform, like a web app to view your stats, like as a customer of F5, having a web app to look at, like a dashboard where you can configure and make changes and see you're getting tons of hits from this angle or this country or whatnot.
Versus the older on-prem solution didn't have anything like that.
They had to like work with an engineer internally to get those numbers.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
No, I can imagine.
So like just for that product, like when you're saying mapping the customer journey, like what that look like?
Yeah, so that's, well, F5 was a big established company when I joined.
So they had a lot of existing workflows, existing metrics.
It was taking stock, doing an audit on like what currently exists, what is.
What is noise to the CS team in relation to the SaaS product?
What can we replicate, work off of, like low-hanging fruit that's already existing?

And it was really just about automating as much as we could for the CSM team throughout the customer journey, whether that's automating QBR, PowerPoint decks using an AI tool called Matic, which is one of my favorite things that I did there.
That tool is like in my top five tech stack for CS now.
It's amazing.
I'll pitch it anywhere I go, basically, at this point.
But yeah, it was like QBR optimization, reporting up to leadership internally for performance of the CSMs across the globe.
It was a 90-person CSM team across all regions.
That was interesting.
How does that reporting...
And again, I totally appreciate Fathom an absolutely gigantic company.
And no one person was doing that whole thing.
I think I'm just trying to like...
You know, get as deep as possible, because I think, you know, just that thinking is helpful, you know, for what we do as well.
So no, like, you know, if that's not a part of what you worked on, like, no pressure.
Oh, no, it definitely is.

It was as granular as working with the engineers in India to build out the data designer features in Gainsight so that the data flowed properly into different metrics within Gainsight, different reports and dashboards, all the way up to the highest level of, you know, how does this thing in the journey align with leadership expectations and the current direction of the CS organization?

So it was really, like, working hand-in-hand with the boots-on-the-ground folks, the CSMs and their managers, and our tooling team, and also being able to communicate what is being done up to leadership at a higher level.
It was kind of an all-interesting thing.
was really, like, my only focus for about six months was, like, this program.
Yeah, yeah.
Well, I'm just looking at Gainsight, but, like, Gainsight is, like, is this the platform my CX team uses?
Is that what it is?
Like, what is, like, what's Gainsight?
Gainsight's like the Ferrari of CRMs.
It's.
Oh, it's CRM.
Is it a CRM more so for, like, existing customers?
Like, they would move out of Salesforce into Gainsight?
Yeah, it's a CS tool through and through.
Everywhere that I've gone that has a CRM has a Salesforce instance, too.
And they're connected and there's, like, deal information, opportunity information that comes into the CS tool.
So that the CSM, when there's a handoff between the teams, the CSM would have more context.
They would know more about the customer from the Salesforce data.
Good.
Okay, so this tool is all the signals, basically.
And then, like, that's what's being kind of.
Yeah, and then you build automation.
You build automation within it.

You have, like, call to actions, tasks, that kind of stuff that the CSM gets alerted for with specific parameters, whether it's, like, a churn indicator or a health score change or something like that.
Right, right.
And did you have any kind of.
Not as much at F5 as I wanted to.
That kind of plays into the not having a seat at the table complaint I had.
But at Ignite, absolutely.
We built, I think, 10 different multidimensional health models using the CRM that they had, which was called Tatango.
And that was very much the team sitting around the table, strategizing and thinking through what are the markers for good health?
How do we weigh these things properly, given a percentage of the total score as a multidimensional weight?

And then how do those flow through the waterfall system that Tatango had specifically, where if a customer met criteria for churn and bad health because of a churn indicator, that was the top of the funnel.
And they couldn't even get a health score for the rest of these things because there's a much more important thing to address right there at the top.
But yeah, very involved with the strategy and tooling of the health scores when I was an analyst at Ignite.
Yeah, that makes sense.
mean, I guess, you know, like, transparent, like, that's the stuff we struggle with.
We're like, I mean, we don't even have a CRM for the post-sale stuff.
Like, we just have one for, like, when we're trying to sell.
Yeah.
trying to use it for the post-sale, but I don't know if it's, like, the right tool.
Is it Salesforce?
No, we use this thing called Adio, which is kind of like a new-age, like, CRM.
ABIO?
ATTIO.
ATTIO.
I'll have to look that up.
I've not heard of that one.

Yeah, but, I mean, I think, like, a lot of our challenges are, like, you know, we have these remote teams that are kind of doing something that normally, like, any agency or even existing company doesn't really do.
Like, are they using kind of a weird suite of tools, working in weird ways, and for us to kind of have.
We'll have.
Thank
into that to say like, hey, we're kind of under delivering on customer A.

Like, you know, to catch that before the customer starts flagging it, it's like kind of challenging because like, you know, bring these signals together and like make an assessment on having that insight.
So, yeah.
And you've got to do like A-B testing so you don't get false flags and red herrings and that kind of stuff as well.
yeah, it's a pretty ambiguous space.
not there.
So, yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, I guess we're kind of just looking for somebody who can come in to help us like kind of like even understand what we need, you know, and then like set it up, obviously.
Yeah.
That sounds like an exciting opportunity.
I did a lot of this kind of stuff around my entrepreneurship program.
There were, you know, business plan competitions, pitch competitions.
I was a coach and judge for multiple years with Seattle U.
University of Washington, kind of working with students through, like, their early stage ideas.
How would you scale this if you got funding?
How would you pitch this to investors to get funding so you can scale, ultimately, to win this competition?
But I like to work with teams that look like they have a little more passion behind it.
Like, they might continue it after the competition, even if they don't win.
And I took on some advisor roles, and I very involved in, like, the young professional community here in Seattle.
I was working on their board, kind of doing, like, operations stuff, hosting events, like, networking events and meetups and stuff like that.
There was a lot more energy back then before COVID around tech startups.
I mean, that community is, like, super important, honestly.
feel like that's what really makes Yeah, your network kind makes you.
Yeah.
Cool.
I think that's great.
mean, did you have any questions for anything?
I mean, you know, open book, happy to share anything.
How do you like it at Growthx, personally?
How do you feel about the company?
Let's see.
I mean, I definitely like it.
trying to think how to articulate why.
mean, I think before Growthx, and even for my own startup, I've built a lot of tools that weren't necessarily for me.

I've built coding tools, dev tools, security tools, and I feel like at Growthx, finally, what I've been doing for myself throughout my career, building tools, automating things, taking advantage of the side of my brain, because I'm probably more technical than most marketers, and then taking advantage of the side where I know the business side.
But I feel like, just like at a meta level, it's a really nice place where I feel like everything's getting used, you know, and I can be myself, and like it's actually...
We were part of even GrowthX's brand.
So that feels great.
And then as a company, I think they've just gotten so many things right, whether totally intentionally or not.

I think with AI, and I was at an AI company before this, back in 2018, when you build tools to let people use, a lot of times it's like, at the current moment with the state of AI, you're giving them something they can shoot themselves in the foot with.
Oh, totally.
It's kind of a lot of work to use something well.
Yeah.
I think what GrowthX gets right is the product they're delivering is not that tool.
It's irrelevant what tool we use.
We're delivering an outcome in the form of a service.
And I think because we've made that decision, it's just going to make the go-to-market so much easier, and it's going to make growth.
So much easier because we don't have to like juggle this thing where we have to like make AI do something that it's really not ready for.
Yeah.

On that note, I read a headline today that Cursor AI, it's like a support agent AI, served a software person at a company like a completely hallucinated policy when he was trying to get support for something.
And he then produced something for his company in line with a fake policy that didn't exist.
And now there's like a lawsuit around it.
I remember it's like shooting yourself in the foot.
I've heard of that before, too.

I've heard in Canada, like one of the airlines, like in the support chat, I think they were using AI and the chat hallucinated some like random discount or deal that didn't exist and they had to like honor it, you know, basically.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
It's good to hear that like growthx is really like a team of people behind it rather than like some developer making an AI that should.
It wouldn't be doing as much as it probably is.
Yeah.
It definitely sounds like it's a lot more about the client relationship and just optimizing the internal workloads and work streams that support that relationship.
Yeah.
I mean, I think that's the best.
You know, I think there is a version of the world where, you know, right now we're really used to kind of self-serve SaaS products.
But I think there's a version of the world where it feels like you're kind of hiring a person.
And whether that person is a real person or, like, some mishmash of, like, AI people and whatever, like, again, it's irrelevant to you.
You hired that person for a job and they're going to do it, you know, and how well they do it is what you're paying for.
Yeah.
And especially right now, it's like, if people aren't keeping up with this, like, aren't keeping up with AI and the progress that you're just falling so far behind.
Yeah.
It's going to eclipse it, honestly.
It's so exponential.
I use OpenAI every single day in my personal life and I've done everything.
Getting from making shopping lists to workout plans to D&D theorycrafting for my Dungeons & Dragons group.
It's really capable.
Where it's supposed to be capable, it's very capable.
That's great.
It sounds like a really cool environment.
I'm obviously pretty energized by early stage and growth stage companies.
Yeah, it seems like a pretty good fit.
But cool.
Yeah, no, I mean, really enjoyed speaking with you.
Your comments about being a more technical marketing person really resonates, too.
Because I feel like I'm way more technical than most of my CS ops people that I work with.
There's a lot of people skill folks in CS, but then you put them on a computer and they don't know how to copy and paste properly.
It's pretty crazy how technologically adept people are and when they should be experts.

I think that's the difficulty, you know, like if somebody tried to make some like AI enabled CS tool, great, but then you give it to kind of a group of people that, you know, for good and bad, like aren't accustomed to thinking or working that way, like in terms of systems, I think that's where kind of problems arise.
Yeah, there's like an accessibility thing to it, like a training and accessibility component.
I had a colleague that was like, he was our digital campaign guy and didn't, he like right clicked everything.
Like he used the context menu for everything on his computer.
didn't use any keyboard shortcuts.
He didn't use like any efficiencies.
Like he had 60 tabs open at any given moment.
Like, yeah, was like, you're our digital guy.
You're our guy charging the digital experience for our customers.
Oh man.
Yeah.
Something's going to break there.
I think, I mean, it's the, I mean, the externality of all this AI stuff is like, I don't really know what happened.
It's when you figure out how to automate those things and the people can't really adapt the same way that the people building can.
Yeah, exactly.
That's why I just try and stay on top of everything.
Yeah.
Cool.
I mean, yeah.
Thanks for your time, Jason.
Yeah, we'll try to see you soon.
And then if you have any questions, feel free to ping me.
Yeah.
Sounds good.
All right.
Appreciate it.
Have a good weekend.
Likewise.
See ya.
Take care.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Exploratory conversation between Jason Gong (GrowthX) and Michael Morrow (potential hire) to discuss Michael's background and potential fit for a customer operations role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Michael has diverse experience in customer operations, from startups (Avanti Markets) to large corporations (F5), with a strong preference for growth-stage environments](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=2.0)
  - [GrowthX is seeking someone to help set up and optimize their post-sale customer operations, including selecting and implementing appropriate tools](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=4.0)
  - [Michael's technical aptitude and experience with CRM/CS tools (e.g., Gainsight) align well with GrowthX's needs](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=47.0)
  - [Both parties expressed mutual interest, with Michael energized by GrowthX's growth stage and AI-focused approach](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=489.0)

## Topics

### Michael's Professional Background

  - [Master's in entrepreneurship, ran small ventures during studies](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=198.0)
  - [First role at Avanti Markets (15 to 35 employees):](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=236.0)
      - [Built new product line (hardware + SaaS)](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=250.0)
      - [Touched all aspects of the business](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=290.0)
  - [Ignite (800 to 1000 employees):](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=372.0)
      - [Focused on data, integrations, and CS tech stack](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=329.0)
  - [F5 Networks:](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=336.0)
      - [Program manager role, less strategic than anticipated](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=348.0)
      - [Built customer journey for SaaS product (DDoS protection)](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
      - [Worked with 90-person global CSM team](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=763.0)

### GrowthX's Current Situation

  - [No dedicated post-sale CRM, using Attio for sales](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=981.0)
  - [Challenges in monitoring remote teams' performance](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=1000.0)
  - [Seeking help to understand needs and set up appropriate systems](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=1053.0)
  - [Delivering outcomes as a service, not just AI tools](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=1247.0)

### Customer Success Tools and Strategies

  - [Gainsight described as the "Ferrari of CRMs" for CS](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=842.0)
  - [Michael's experience with health scoring models:](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=923.0)
      - [Built 10 multidimensional health models at Ignite](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=923.0)
      - [Limited input on health scoring at F5](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=348.0)
  - [Automation within CS tools:](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)
      - [Building call-to-actions, tasks based on specific parameters](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)
      - [QBR optimization using AI tool Matic](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)

### AI in Customer Operations

  - [GrowthX focusing on delivering outcomes, not just AI tools](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=1239.0)
  - [Challenges of AI hallucinations in customer-facing scenarios](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=1268.0)
  - [Importance of human oversight in AI-driven processes](https://fathom.video/share/cnqgwMiskiKsMD7ASsRaaYnSz4ojguys?tab=summary&timestamp=1297.0)

## Next Steps

  - Michael to follow up with any additional questions
  - Potential further discussions on specific role and responsibilities at GrowthX
  - GrowthX to evaluate Michael's fit for their customer operations needs
